questions.question: Why does green exercise have the strongest combined evidence base in nature-based mental wellness?
options: It combines robust exercise science (strong evidence) with nature-specific additive effects (moderate evidence)
It only works in pristine wilderness areas unavailable to most people
It requires professional supervision like adventure therapy
It has larger effect sizes than all established psychological interventions
questions.explanation: Green exercise combines well-established exercise benefits (strong evidence from decades of research) with moderate evidence for nature-specific additive effects. This dual mechanism produces large effect sizes (SMD = −0.71 for anxiety, −0.72 for fatigue) and makes it the most action-ready, evidence-supported nature-based practice.

questions.question: What is the MINIMUM effective dose of green exercise for mood and self-esteem improvement, according to Barton & Pretty (2010)?
options: 60 minutes minimum for any benefit
30-45 minutes at moderate-to-vigorous intensity
5 minutes of green exercise produces significant improvements
2 hours of wilderness hiking required
questions.explanation: The landmark Barton & Pretty (2010) dose-response study found that even 5-minute bouts of green exercise produced significant mood and self-esteem improvements. The benefit curve peaks around 20–30 minutes with diminishing returns beyond 60 minutes. This means brief, frequent green exercise is highly effective — you don't need hour-long sessions.

questions.question: What makes 'blue space' (aquatic environments) particularly effective for green exercise?
options: Blue space has no advantage over terrestrial green space
Blue space activities (ocean, lakes, rivers) produce the largest psychological effect sizes in green exercise research
Blue space only works if you're swimming, not walking near water
Blue space is only effective in tropical climates
questions.explanation: Aquatic environments (blue space) produce the largest psychological effect sizes in green exercise research (Barton & Pretty, 2010). Coastal and lake proximity is associated with lower depression and higher wellbeing. Proposed mechanisms include visual fascination (water movement, reflections), soundscape (waves masking urban noise), and evolutionary salience of water sources. Even water-adjacent activities (beach walking, kayaking) show stronger restoration than terrestrial settings.

questions.question: Does nature provide additive psychological benefits beyond indoor exercise, according to meta-analytic evidence?
options: No — all benefits come from movement; nature setting makes no difference
Yes — nature settings show significantly lower anxiety and depression than urban or indoor exercise for the same activity
Nature only helps if you exercise for more than one hour
The evidence is mixed and inconclusive
questions.explanation: Meta-analyses show nature provides additive psychological benefits: nature walking reduces anxiety (SMD = −0.71) and depression more than urban walking; nature settings outperform indoor exercise for mood and positive affect. A 2025 meta-analysis found nature walking associated with significantly lower anxiety and heart rate than city walking. Movement matters most, but nature adds measurable supplementary benefit.

questions.question: What predicts adherence to green exercise better than prescribed intensity or duration?
options: Having expensive outdoor gear and equipment
Living within walking distance of a national park
Preference-based activity (choosing what you enjoy) and starting with sustainable doses
Vigorous intensity exercise regardless of enjoyment
questions.explanation: Preference-based activity (choosing green exercise you actually enjoy) predicts adherence better than prescribed intensity. Light-to-moderate intensity activities (walking, leisurely cycling, gardening) show strongest sustained benefits with lowest dropout rates. Starting small and specific ('15 minutes in [specific park] after work on Tuesdays') works better than generic goals. The best green exercise is the one you'll do regularly.
